comment_text.snp2.14:5.1: At DN 20:11.7 Erāvaṇa is clearly a dragon, but later tradition depicted him as a great elephant, the mount of Sakka.
comment_text.snp2.14:6.1: Kuvera is identified as Vessavaṇa at DN 32:7.43.
comment_text.snp2.14:7.1: While titthiya is commonly found in the Vinaya, this is the only place it occurs in the suttas that is not part of a stock list including kings, etc., or compounded with añña- or nānā-.
comment_text.snp2.14:11.3: Atthadaso: Bodhi follows comm in “seeing the good”, Norman has “seeing the goal”. I usually have “seeing the meaning”, and I think that is supported here. It follows several lines talking about the teaching. A reflective person doesn't just hear the teaching, but understands the point of it and puts it into practice.

root_text.snp2.14:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.snp2.14:1.3: Atha kho dhammiko upāsako pañcahi upāsakasatehi saddhiṁ yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho dhammiko upāsako bhagavantaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.snp2.14:13.1: Rūpā ca saddā ca rasā ca gandhā,
root_text.snp2.14:20.1: Pāṇaṁ na hane na ca ghātayeyya,
root_text.snp2.14:20.4: Ye thāvarā ye ca tasā santi loke.
root_text.snp2.14:26.2: Musā na bhāse na ca majjapo siyā;
root_text.snp2.14:27.1: Mālaṁ na dhāre na ca gandhamācare,

translation_text.snp2.14:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.snp2.14:1.3: Then the lay follower Dhammika, together with five hundred lay followers, went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.snp2.14:2.1: “I ask you, Gotama, whose wisdom is vast:
translation_text.snp2.14:2.2: what does one do to become a good disciple,
translation_text.snp2.14:2.3: both one who has left the home,
translation_text.snp2.14:2.4: and the lay followers staying at home?
translation_text.snp2.14:3.1: For you understand the course and destiny
translation_text.snp2.14:3.2: of the world with all its gods.
translation_text.snp2.14:3.3: There is no equal to you who sees the subtle meaning,
translation_text.snp2.14:3.4: for you are the Buddha most excellent, they say.
translation_text.snp2.14:4.2: you explain the teaching out of sympathy for beings.
translation_text.snp2.14:4.3: All-seer, you have cast off the veil,
translation_text.snp2.14:4.4: and immaculate, you shine on the whole world.
translation_text.snp2.14:5.1: The dragon king Erāvaṇa, <j>hearing you called ‘Victor’,
translation_text.snp2.14:5.3: He consulted with you then, <j>having heard your words,
translation_text.snp2.14:6.2: approached to ask about the teaching.
translation_text.snp2.14:6.3: You also answered him, O attentive one,
translation_text.snp2.14:6.4: and hearing you he too was consoled.
translation_text.snp2.14:7.1: Those monastics of other religions given to debate,
translation_text.snp2.14:7.3: all fail to overtake you in wisdom,
translation_text.snp2.14:7.4: like a standing man next to a sprinter.
translation_text.snp2.14:8.2: some of whom are quite senior,
translation_text.snp2.14:8.3: all end up beholden to you for the meaning,
translation_text.snp2.14:8.4: and others too who think themselves debaters.
translation_text.snp2.14:9.1: So subtle and pleasant is the teaching
translation_text.snp2.14:9.2: that is well proclaimed by you, Blessed One.
translation_text.snp2.14:9.3: It’s all we long to hear. So when asked,
translation_text.snp2.14:9.4: O Best of Buddhas, tell us!
translation_text.snp2.14:10.2: and the layfolk too are here to listen.
translation_text.snp2.14:10.3: Let them hear the teaching <j>the immaculate one discovered,
translation_text.snp2.14:10.4: like gods listening to the fine words of Vāsava.”
translation_text.snp2.14:11.1: “Listen to me, mendicants, I will educate you
translation_text.snp2.14:11.2: in the cleansing teaching; all bear it in mind.
translation_text.snp2.14:11.3: An intelligent person, seeing the meaning,
translation_text.snp2.14:11.4: would adopt the deportment <j>proper to a renunciate.
translation_text.snp2.14:12.1: No way would a mendicant go out at the wrong time;
translation_text.snp2.14:12.2: at the right time, they’d walk the village for alms.
translation_text.snp2.14:12.3: For chains bind one who wanders at the wrong time,
translation_text.snp2.14:12.4: which is why the Buddhas avoid it.
translation_text.snp2.14:13.1: Sights, sounds, tastes, smells, and touches,
translation_text.snp2.14:13.4: and enter for the morning meal at the right time.
translation_text.snp2.14:14.1: After receiving alms for the day,
translation_text.snp2.14:14.2: on returning a mendicant would sit in private alone.
translation_text.snp2.14:14.3: Inwardly reflective, they’d curb their mind
translation_text.snp2.14:14.4: from outside things, keeping themselves collected.
translation_text.snp2.14:15.1: Should they converse with a disciple,
translation_text.snp2.14:15.2: with anyone else, or with a mendicant,
translation_text.snp2.14:15.3: they’d bring up only the sublime teaching,
translation_text.snp2.14:16.2: but we praise not those of little wisdom.
translation_text.snp2.14:16.3: In place after place they are bound in chains,
translation_text.snp2.14:16.4: for they send their mind over there far away.
translation_text.snp2.14:17.1: Alms, a dwelling, a bed and seat,
translation_text.snp2.14:17.2: and water for rinsing the dust from the cloak—
translation_text.snp2.14:17.3: after hearing the teaching of the Holy One,
translation_text.snp2.14:17.4: a disciple of outstanding wisdom would use these after appraisal.
translation_text.snp2.14:18.1: That’s why, when it comes to alms and lodgings,
translation_text.snp2.14:18.2: and water for rinsing the dust from the cloak,
translation_text.snp2.14:18.3: a mendicant is unsullied in the midst of these things,
translation_text.snp2.14:18.4: like a droplet on a lotus-leaf.
translation_text.snp2.14:19.1: Now I shall tell you the householder’s duty,
translation_text.snp2.14:19.2: doing which one becomes a good disciple.
translation_text.snp2.14:19.3: For one burdened with possessions <j>does not get to realize
translation_text.snp2.14:19.4: the whole of the mendicant’s practice.
translation_text.snp2.14:20.1: They’d not kill any creature, nor have them killed,
translation_text.snp2.14:20.2: nor grant permission for others to kill.
translation_text.snp2.14:20.3: They’ve laid aside violence towards all creatures
translation_text.snp2.14:20.4: frail or firm that there are in the world.
translation_text.snp2.14:21.1: Next, a disciple would avoid knowingly
translation_text.snp2.14:21.2: taking anything not given at all,
translation_text.snp2.14:21.3: they’d not get others to do it, <j>nor grant them permission to steal;
translation_text.snp2.14:22.1: A sensible person would avoid the unchaste life,
translation_text.snp2.14:22.2: like a burning pit of coals.
translation_text.snp2.14:22.3: But if unable to remain chaste,
translation_text.snp2.14:22.4: they’d not transgress with another’s partner.
translation_text.snp2.14:23.2: or one on one, they would not lie.
translation_text.snp2.14:23.3: They’d not get others to lie, <j>nor grant them permission to lie;
translation_text.snp2.14:24.2: would not consume liquor or drink.
translation_text.snp2.14:24.3: They’d not get others to drink, <j>nor grant them permission to drink;
translation_text.snp2.14:25.1: For drunken fools do bad things,
translation_text.snp2.14:25.4: the maddening, deluding frolic of fools.
translation_text.snp2.14:26.1: You shouldn’t kill living creatures, or steal,
translation_text.snp2.14:26.4: and don’t eat at night, the wrong time.
translation_text.snp2.14:27.1: Not wearing garlands or applying fragrance,
translation_text.snp2.14:27.2: you should sleep on a low bed, <j>or a mat on the ground.
translation_text.snp2.14:27.3: This is the eight-factored sabbath, they say,
translation_text.snp2.14:27.4: explained by the Buddha, <j>who has gone to suffering’s end.
translation_text.snp2.14:28.1: Then having rightly undertaken the sabbath
translation_text.snp2.14:28.2: complete in all its eight factors
translation_text.snp2.14:28.3: on the fourteenth, fifteenth, <j>and eighth of the fortnight,
translation_text.snp2.14:28.4: as well as on the fortnightly special displays,
translation_text.snp2.14:29.2: a clever person, rejoicing with confident heart,
translation_text.snp2.14:29.4: to the mendicant Saṅgha as is fitting.
translation_text.snp2.14:30.1: One should rightfully support one’s parents,
translation_text.snp2.14:30.3: A diligent layperson observing these duties
translation_text.snp2.14:30.4: ascends to the gods called Self-luminous.”

variant_text.snp2.14:31.3: Dhammacariyañca brāhmaṇo → kapilaṁca puna brāhmaṇadhammikaṁ (bj); kapilo brāhmaṇopi ca (sya-all, mr); dhammacariyā puna brāhmaṇadhammikaṁ (pts-vp-pli1)
